threats.title: Critical Missing Authorization in updateOrder Allows Any User to Modify Any Active Order
threats.hypothesis_description: The updateOrder function in MarginTradingHook checks that the caller has a valid position (initPosId != 0) but never verifies that the order being updated belongs to the caller's position. Specifically, it omits the check `order.initPosId == initPosId` which IS present in the analogous cancelOrder function. Any user with any valid margin position can modify any other user's active order parameters including triggerPrice_e36, limitPrice_e36, collAmt, and tokenOut.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code-level access control bypass visible in the source. The cancelOrder function at the same contract includes the guard `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT)` but updateOrder does not. This enables a multi-step attack: (1) attacker creates their own position, (2) attacker calls updateOrder with their posId but victim's orderId to set limitPrice to an extreme value favoring the attacker, (3) attacker calls fillOrder to execute the modified order, receiving the victim's collateral at a manipulated price.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateOrder reads initPosId from caller's mapping and checks it != 0, but never asserts order.initPosId == initPosId. Compare with cancelOrder at lines 276-283 which does assert this.

preconditions: Victim has at least one active order (stop-loss or take-profit)
Attacker has any valid margin position (can open a minimal one)
Attacker has enough tokens to act as order executor in fillOrder

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: updateOrder at line 362 checks `initPosId != 0` for caller but never validates `order.initPosId == initPosId` unlike cancelOrder at line 280
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - this is exploitable by any unprivileged on-chain actor
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - any user with any active order is vulnerable at all times

operational_questions: Can the attacker set limitPrice_e36 to extract maximum collateral via fillOrder?
Can tokenOut be changed to a worthless token to reduce attacker's cost?
Does the _calculateFillOrderInfo correctly compute amtOut with attacker-chosen parameters?
validation_requirements: Write PoC: create position A (victim) with order, create position B (attacker), call updateOrder(B_posId, A_orderId, ...) to modify victim's order
Verify modified order can be filled by attacker to extract victim's collateral
Calculate maximum extractable value per attack

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker opens minimal margin position to get a valid posId
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker identifies victim's active orderId (from events or getPosOrderIds)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls updateOrder(attackerPosId, victimOrderId, extremeTriggerPrice, attacker-favorable limitPrice, maxCollAmt, cheapTokenOut)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker calls fillOrder(victimOrderId) providing minimal borrToken repay and cheap tokenOut, receiving victim's real collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single attacker, single transaction sequence, no coordination needed

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete theft of collateral from all users with active margin orders
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All margin order users are at risk; trust in the order system is destroyed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to patch updateOrder; stolen funds are unrecoverable
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All stop-loss and take-profit orders in the margin trading system

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: No timing constraints - attack works at any time

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: updateOrder events where the caller's position doesn't match the order's position
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Orders being updated with extreme price parameters
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Orders being filled immediately after parameter changes by different addresses

threats.title: Return Value Swap in _calculateRepaySize Causes fillOrder to Use Incorrect Amounts/Shares
threats.hypothesis_description: The _calculateRepaySize function returns (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares) in that positional order, but _calculateFillOrderInfo destructures the return as (repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...), swapping the two values. This causes fillOrder to use the token amount where share count is expected and vice versa. Once interest accrues (making shares != amounts), all order fills become broken or produce incorrect economic outcomes.
threats.investigation_rationale: Solidity return value assignment is positional. _calculateRepaySize returns position 0 = repayAmt (token amount), position 1 = repayShares (share count). The destructuring in _calculateFillOrderInfo assigns position 0 to local 'repayShares' and position 1 to local 'repayAmt', swapping the semantics. In fillOrder, the executor transfers 'repayAmt' (actually shares) of borrToken, and InitCore.repay is called with 'repayShares' (actually token amount) as shares parameter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _calculateRepaySize signature returns (uint repayAmt, uint repayShares) at line 458, but _calculateFillOrderInfo at line 395 destructures as (repayShares, repayAmt) = _calculateRepaySize(...), swapping position 0 and position 1

preconditions: Any pool with interest accrued (share/amount ratio != 1:1)
Any active margin order exists on such a pool
An executor attempts to fill the order

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: fillOrder calls _calculateFillOrderInfo which destructures _calculateRepaySize return values in swapped order
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - this is a code-level logic error affecting all order fills
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once any interest accrues on any lending pool with margin orders

operational_questions: Does the swapped values cause reverts (DoS) or incorrect economic outcomes (loss)?
When debtShareToAmt ratio is 1:1, does the bug manifest?
Can an attacker profit from the incorrect computation?
validation_requirements: Write test: create margin position, create order, accrue interest, attempt fillOrder
Verify that the executor transfers wrong amount of borrToken
Verify that repay is called with wrong share count
Check if amtOut calculation is also corrupted

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Interest accrues on any pool (happens naturally over time)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: repayShares (in _calculateFillOrderInfo) now holds token amount > actual shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: fillOrder: executor transfers repayAmt (actually shares, smaller number) of borrToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: repay called with repayShares (actually amount, larger number) → tries to repay more shares than hook has tokens for → reverts OR extracts incorrect collateral amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: No coordination needed - the bug affects all order fills automatically once interest diverges

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All margin order fills are broken or produce incorrect results
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Stop-loss and take-profit orders become non-functional, exposing users to unlimited losses
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade; existing orders cannot be properly filled
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: fillOrder for all margin trading orders

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None - the bug is in the code logic

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed fillOrder transactions or fillOrder calls with incorrect token transfer amounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All fillOrder calls reverting on pools with accrued interest
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users unable to have their stop-loss/take-profit orders executed

threats.title: fillOrder Auto-Cancel Only Modifies Memory, Leaving Order Permanently Active in Storage
threats.hypothesis_description: In fillOrder, when position collateral is 0, the function loads the order into memory (`Order memory order = __orders[_orderId]`), sets `order.status = OrderStatus.Cancelled` on the memory copy, emits CancelOrder, and returns. However, the storage mapping `__orders[_orderId]` is never updated, so the order remains Active in storage. This means the order can be filled later if collateral is re-added, or fillOrder can be called repeatedly wasting gas and emitting misleading events.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code at fillOrder line 291 reads `Order memory order = __orders[_orderId]` creating a memory copy. Line 296 sets `order.status = OrderStatus.Cancelled` on the memory copy only. Compare with cancelOrder at line 281 which correctly uses `Order storage order = __orders[_orderId]`. The storage order remains Active, contradicting the emitted CancelOrder event.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Order memory order = __orders[_orderId]; ... order.status = OrderStatus.Cancelled; — modifies memory copy, not storage. Compare cancelOrder which uses Order storage order.

preconditions: A margin position with an active order has its collateral fully removed (e.g., via liquidation or reducePos)
fillOrder is called on that order

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: fillOrder uses 'memory' keyword for order loading while cancelOrder uses 'storage' — inconsistent storage mutation pattern
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any caller can trigger this by calling fillOrder when position has 0 collateral
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for all orders where position collateral reaches 0

operational_questions: Can the uncancelled order be filled after collateral is re-added?
Does this create unexpected loss for users who see CancelOrder event but later have their order filled?
Can this be weaponized via the updateOrder auth bypass (OPS-H001)?
validation_requirements: Test: create order, remove all collateral, call fillOrder, verify storage status is still Active
Test: re-add collateral, call fillOrder again, verify order gets filled unexpectedly
Verify gas griefing by calling fillOrder repeatedly

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify victim order on a position whose collateral was removed (via liquidation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call fillOrder → CancelOrder event emitted, victim believes order is cancelled
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for victim to re-add collateral to the position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call fillOrder again → order fills unexpectedly using the re-added collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Minimal - just monitoring for collateral re-addition

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Users' orders execute at unexpected times after they believe cancellation occurred
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Order status tracking is broken for any order that goes through auto-cancel path
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Contract upgrade needed to fix; affected orders remain in incorrect state
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All margin trading orders that encounter the auto-cancel path

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: CancelOrder events followed by FillOrder events on the same orderId
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Orders being filled after appearing cancelled in event logs
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated fillOrder calls on same orderId with 0 collateral

threats.title: CloseExactOut Strict Equality Check Enables 1-Wei Griefing DoS on Position Reduction
threats.hypothesis_description: In MarginTradingHook.coreCallback, the CloseExactOut path uses a strict equality check: `_require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL)`. An attacker can front-run any reducePos transaction that uses CloseExactOut by sending 1 wei of the borrow token (tokenOut) to the hook contract, causing the balance check to fail since balance = amtOut_from_swap + 1_wei_dust != amtOut.
threats.investigation_rationale: The strict equality `==` check at the coreCallback function is brittle. Since the hook's address is publicly known (deployed proxy), any attacker can send 1 wei of any borrow token to the hook. When a user's reducePos triggers a CloseExactOut swap, the hook's borrow token balance after swap = legitimate_amtOut + 1_wei_dust, which fails the `== swapInfo.amtOut` check, reverting the entire transaction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (swapInfo.swapType == SwapType.CloseExactOut) { _require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL); } — strict equality with balanceOf is griefable

preconditions: Hook contract is deployed (address is public)
Any user attempts reducePos with tokenOut == collToken (triggering CloseExactOut)
Attacker sends 1 wei of the borrow token to the hook address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Strict equality check on balanceOf is fragile — should use >= instead of ==
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any address can send dust tokens to the hook
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Persistent once dust is sent; affects all subsequent CloseExactOut operations for that borrow token

operational_questions: Is the dust cleared by other operations (e.g., refundNative)?
Can the attacker target specific borrow tokens to block specific position closures?
Is there any natural dust accumulation that makes this self-triggering?
validation_requirements: Send 1 wei of borrow token to hook, then attempt reducePos with CloseExactOut, verify revert
Verify that OpenExactIn operations are NOT affected (they use >= implicitly via transfer)
Test if dust gets swept by subsequent operations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target borrow tokens used in margin positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Send 1 wei of each borrow token to the MarginTradingHook proxy address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All reducePos operations using CloseExactOut for those tokens now revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users cannot close positions using collateral token as output, potentially trapping them during market crashes
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single transaction per borrow token, trivial cost

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Users unable to reduce positions during market downturns
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Critical position management functionality blocked for negligible cost
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Dust cannot be easily removed without contract upgrade; users must use CloseExactIn as workaround
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All reducePos operations using tokenOut == collateral token

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Can be set up persistently or targeted at specific times

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Small token transfers to the MarginTradingHook address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: reducePos transactions reverting with SLIPPAGE_CONTROL error
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Dust deposits preceding user position reduction attempts

threats.title: reducePos Missing Position Existence Validation Allows Operating on Zero-Address Margin Position
threats.hypothesis_description: The reducePos function does not validate that initPosIds[msg.sender][_posId] != 0 before proceeding, unlike increasePos, addCollateral, removeCollateral, and repayDebt which all include this check. When called with a non-existent posId, initPosId defaults to 0, and _reducePosInternal reads __marginPositions[0] which contains zero addresses. While subsequent external calls to address(0) pools would likely revert, the error message is misleading and the inconsistent validation is a code defect.
threats.investigation_rationale: Comparing reducePos with other position-modifying functions reveals a missing `_require(initPosId != 0, Errors.POSITION_NOT_FOUND)` check. This is inconsistent with the pattern established by increasePos (line 119), addCollateral (line 163), removeCollateral (line 178), and repayDebt (line 196).

proof_of_signal.pattern: reducePos reads initPosId = initPosIds[msg.sender][_posId] without checking != 0, while increasePos at line 119 includes _require(initPosId != 0, Errors.POSITION_NOT_FOUND)

preconditions: User calls reducePos with a posId that doesn't exist in their mapping

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing validation guard present in all sibling functions

operational_questions: Does __marginPositions[0] ever get populated (could someone openPos and get posId mapping to initPosId 0)?
Could the revert on address(0) pool calls be exploited in any way?
Does this interact with the multicall pattern to bypass any checks?
validation_requirements: Call reducePos with non-existent posId, verify revert behavior
Verify no position can have initPosId == 0 in normal flow

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call reducePos with arbitrary _posId that doesn't map to any position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Subsequent calls to ILendingPool(address(0)) revert with unhelpful error
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No direct value extraction but confusing error propagation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Misleading error messages when operating on non-existent positions

threats.title: LP Price Manipulation via Flash Loan When kLast Is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: WLpMoeMasterChef.calculatePrice_e36 uses spot reserves (r0, r1) when kLast == 0. While the sqrt formula provides some manipulation resistance for single swaps (since r0*r1 ≈ constant), repeated back-and-forth swaps within a single transaction accumulate swap fees, increasing k = r0*r1 and thus inflating the LP price. An attacker could use this to inflate wrapped LP collateral value, borrow against it, and leave the protocol with bad debt.
threats.investigation_rationale: When kLast == 0 (Moe pair fee switch off): price = 2*sqrt(r0*p0)*sqrt(r1*p1)/totalSupply = 2*sqrt(r0*r1*p0*p1)/totalSupply. Each swap adds fees to reserves, incrementally increasing r0*r1. Multiple flash-funded swaps in one tx can accumulate this increase. The kLast != 0 branch uses the stored kLast which is NOT updated during swaps, making it resistant. The project documentation explicitly flags LP price manipulation as a risk area.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (kLast == 0) { price = 2 * (r0 * prices_e36[0]).sqrt() * (r1 * prices_e36[1]).sqrt() / totalSupply; } — uses live reserves r0,r1 from getReserves()

preconditions: Moe pair has kLast == 0 (fee switch is off)
The wrapped LP token is used as collateral in a position
Attacker has access to flash loan capital to execute repeated swaps

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: calculatePrice_e36 uses spot reserves when kLast == 0, which are modifiable within a single transaction
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: kLast state depends on Moe protocol governance (fee switch)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever kLast == 0 for any Moe pair used as wrapped LP collateral

operational_questions: What is the current kLast state for Moe pairs used as collateral?
How much can sqrt(r0*r1) increase with N repeated swaps given fee rate?
Is the price inflation sufficient to exceed collateral factor protection?
validation_requirements: Simulate flash loan + repeated swap attack on a Moe pair with kLast == 0
Measure LP price inflation achievable within one transaction
Calculate profitability: price inflation * collateral value - flash loan fees - swap fees

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Flash loan large amount of token0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Execute multiple swap cycles in the Moe pair to accumulate fees in reserves
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: LP price is inflated; borrow maximum against wrapped LP collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Reverse swaps and repay flash loan; keep borrowed assets as profit
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single atomic transaction via flash loan

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Protocol accumulates bad debt from undercollateralized positions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lenders lose funds if bad debt exceeds reserves
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Bad debt is permanent; requires socialized losses or treasury intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All positions using wrapped LP as collateral on affected pairs

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Moderate - requires flash loan and multi-swap orchestration

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large flash loans followed by multiple swaps on Moe pairs with LP collateral
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden spikes in wrapped LP collateral value followed by large borrows
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Positions opened and maximally leveraged in single transactions

threats.title: Multiple Orders Over-Commit Collateral Without Aggregate Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: When creating margin orders via _createOrder, the function checks that _collAmt <= current position collateral individually per order but never validates the aggregate collAmt across all active orders for a position. A user can create multiple orders that collectively exceed their total collateral. When filled sequentially, later orders find insufficient collateral and _calculateRepaySize scales down repayShares proportionally, producing different effective execution prices than the user configured.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _createOrder, the check is `_require(_collAmt <= collAmt, Errors.INPUT_TOO_HIGH)` where collAmt is the TOTAL position collateral. Two orders each with collAmt = totalCollateral would pass individually but together commit 2x the available collateral. When the first order fills, it uses the full collateral, and the second order would find 0 or reduced collateral, causing _calculateRepaySize to scale down proportionally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _createOrder checks _collAmt <= collAmt (individual check only, no aggregate), _calculateRepaySize silently caps order.collAmt at totalCollAmt

preconditions: User creates multiple stop-loss or take-profit orders on the same position
Combined collAmt across orders exceeds position's total collateral

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No sum-of-collAmts check across active orders for a position
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever multiple orders exist on the same position

operational_questions: What is the effective execution price when _calculateRepaySize scales down collAmt?
Can a user accidentally lose money from unexpected proportional scaling?
Can an attacker use this with OPS-H001 to further manipulate outcomes?
validation_requirements: Create 2 orders each committing full collateral, fill first, verify second has incorrect execution
Calculate value difference between expected and actual fill amounts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User creates stop-loss order A with collAmt = totalColl
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User creates take-profit order B with collAmt = totalColl
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Order A fills, consuming all collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Order B: _calculateRepaySize caps collAmt to 0, repayShares = 0, amtOut = collTokenAmt(0) - 0 = 0; user gets nothing
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - this is a user-facing accounting inconsistency

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Users receive less value than expected from order fills
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Orders execute with different parameters than configured
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - user education or code fix
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Margin order creation and fill system

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple active orders on same position with sum(collAmt) > total collateral
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Order fills with 0 or near-0 amtOut
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users creating overlapping stop-loss and take-profit orders

threats.title: Underwater Positions Cannot Be Liquidated Due to Zero Incentive Multiplier at Health Factor Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: LiqIncentiveCalculator.getLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 returns 0 when _healthFactor_e18 == 0 (completely underwater position). In InitCore.liquidate, the liquidation incentive multiplier of 0 means repayAmtWithLiqIncentive = repayAmt * 0 / 1e18 = 0, resulting in 0 collateral shares returned to the liquidator. No rational liquidator would execute this since they repay debt but receive nothing. This creates permanent bad debt that can never be cleared through the liquidation mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The early return `if (_healthFactor_e18 == 0) return 0;` in getLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 is mathematically consistent with the formula (1/0 would overflow), but creates an economic dead-end. The InitCore.liquidate function bypasses post-liquidation health checks for health_e18 == 0, correctly recognizing the underwater state, but fails to provide adequate incentive for liquidators to clear the bad debt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_healthFactor_e18 == 0) return 0; — returns zero multiplier for underwater positions

preconditions: A position reaches health factor == 0 (collateral credit drops to 0 while debt remains)
This can occur when collFactor for a token is set to 0, or when collateral value drops to 0 while debt exists

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: getLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 returns 0 for healthFactor==0, making liquidation economically irrational
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires collateral value to drop to 0 or collFactor to be set to 0
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever any position reaches exactly 0 health factor

operational_questions: How likely is health factor reaching exactly 0 vs approaching 0?
Does the minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 override the 0 return?
Is there a governance mechanism to handle bad debt outside liquidation?
validation_requirements: Create position, manipulate oracle or factors so health reaches 0, attempt liquidation
Verify liquidator receives 0 collateral despite repaying debt
Check if minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 is applied before or after the early return

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Open leveraged position on volatile asset
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Asset price crashes to 0 or collFactor is reduced to 0 → health factor reaches exactly 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Position becomes unliquidatable — no rational liquidator will repay debt for 0 collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bad debt permanently remains in the system, eroding lender value
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Extreme market conditions or governance action on collateral factors

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Bad debt accumulation degrades pool solvency
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lender trust eroded as bad debt cannot be cleared
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governance intervention or socialized losses

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low but requires extreme market conditions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Positions with health factor == 0 that persist over time without liquidation
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Growing bad debt in lending pools
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Liquidation attempts that repay debt but receive 0 collateral

threats.title: InitOracle Reverts When Both Sources Valid but maxPriceDeviation Not Set (Default 0)
threats.hypothesis_description: When a token has both primary and secondary oracle sources configured and both return valid prices, InitOracle.getPrice_e36 checks `(maxPrice_e36 * ONE_E18) / minPrice_e36 <= maxPriceDeviations_e18[_token]`. If maxPriceDeviations_e18 was never set for the token (defaults to 0), this check always fails since any non-zero price ratio > 0. This creates a DoS on price fetching for that token, blocking all operations dependent on its price (borrowing, liquidation, health checks).
threats.investigation_rationale: maxPriceDeviations_e18 mapping defaults to 0 for unset tokens. The deviation check at line 69-72 compares ratio against 0, which always fails for valid positive prices. Adding a secondary source without setting deviation creates an unexpected DoS. The setMaxPriceDeviations_e18 function requires values >= 1e18, confirming 0 is never a valid deviation.
deployment_assumptions: Governor sets secondary source without also setting maxPriceDeviation for the same token
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script provided to verify initialization sequence; InitOracle.initialize() is empty, and setSecondarySources / setMaxPriceDeviations_e18 are separate governor calls

proof_of_signal.pattern: maxPriceDeviations_e18[_token] defaults to 0; deviation check: (maxPrice * 1e18) / minPrice <= 0 always fails for positive prices

preconditions: Governor sets both primary and secondary sources for a token
Governor does NOT set maxPriceDeviations_e18 for the same token
Both sources return valid prices

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setSecondarySources and setMaxPriceDeviations_e18 are separate calls with no enforced coupling
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From the moment secondary source is added until maxPriceDeviation is set

operational_questions: Is there a deployment script that atomically sets sources and deviations?
Can the governor add secondary source and deviation in a multicall?
What operations are blocked when oracle reverts for a token?
validation_requirements: Set primary and secondary sources without deviation, verify getPrice_e36 reverts
Map cascade of failures: health checks → liquidations → borrowing all blocked

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governor adds secondary source for token X without setting deviation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Both oracle sources return valid prices
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: getPrice_e36(tokenX) reverts due to deviation check failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All operations involving tokenX price are blocked: no health checks, no liquidations, no borrows

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Token-level DoS on all protocol operations involving the affected token
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Positions with the affected token cannot be liquidated, potentially accumulating bad debt
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Governor must set maxPriceDeviation; if timelock exists, delay could be significant
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All price-dependent operations for the affected token

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Tokens with secondarySource set but maxPriceDeviation == 0
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: getPrice_e36 reverts for tokens with two valid oracle sources
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Governance transactions setting sources without deviations

threats.title: coreCallback Sweeps Entire Token Balance to SwapHelper Including Residual Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In MarginTradingHook.coreCallback, the function transfers the ENTIRE balance of tokenIn held by the hook to the swapHelper: `uint amtIn = IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this)); IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).safeTransfer(swapHelper, amtIn)`. If the hook holds residual tokenIn from prior operations, rounding, or donations, these tokens are included in the current user's swap. This could benefit the next user (receiving extra swap output) or be used to manipulate swap amounts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The hook does not track expected token amounts per operation. Instead, it uses balanceOf(this) to determine the swap input amount. Any pre-existing balance of tokenIn in the hook gets swept into the swap. Combined with the multicall pattern where the hook receives borrowed tokens and needs to swap them, this could lead to accounting mismatches.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint amtIn = IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this)); IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).safeTransfer(swapHelper, amtIn); — uses entire balance, not operation-specific amount

preconditions: Hook contract holds residual tokenIn (from prior operation, donation, or rounding)
User calls openPos/increasePos triggering coreCallback

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Balance-of pattern instead of tracking exact operation amounts
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever residual tokens exist in the hook

operational_questions: Can residual tokens accumulate naturally from rounding in prior operations?
Can an attacker donate tokens to inflate a victim's swap input?
Does this interact with the strict equality check in CloseExactOut?
validation_requirements: Send residual tokenIn to hook, execute openPos, verify extra tokens are included in swap
Calculate economic impact of residual token inclusion

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify periods when hook holds residual tokens (after partial operations or rounding)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Next user's openPos/increasePos sweeps residual tokens into their swap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Extra swap output goes to the user's collateral position (benefits the user, not the attacker)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Or: attacker donates wrong-direction tokens to confuse swap routing

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Accounting inconsistency between expected and actual swap amounts
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Minor - generally benefits the next user rather than harming them
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - fix by tracking per-operation amounts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Hook contract holding unexpected token balances between transactions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Swap amounts larger than expected borrowing amounts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Token donations to hook address followed by margin operations

threats.title: Min Liquidation Incentive Can Exceed Max Due to Missing Validation in Setter
threats.hypothesis_description: LiqIncentiveCalculator.setMinLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 does not validate that the min multiplier is <= maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18. In getLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18, the final computation is `multiplier_e18 = Math.max(Math.min(ONE_E18 + incentive, max), min)`. When min > max, Math.max selects min, effectively bypassing the max cap and allowing liquidation incentives higher than intended. This over-incentivizes liquidation, allowing liquidators to extract excess collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: The setMinLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 function at LiqIncentiveCalculator has no bound check against maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18. The computation at line 45 applies min after max: Math.min caps at max, then Math.max raises back to min, meaning min > max results in min winning.
deployment_assumptions: Governor sets minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 > maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 for some mode
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script showing initial values; setMinLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 has no upper bound validation against maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18

proof_of_signal.pattern: Math.max(Math.min(value, max), min) where min can exceed max; setMinLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 has no validation against maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18

preconditions: Governor sets minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18[mode] > maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 (misconfiguration)
A position in that mode becomes liquidatable

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No validation that min <= max in setter functions

operational_questions: What are the current values of min and max incentive multipliers?
Could a compromised governor intentionally set min > max to drain positions?
What is the maximum extractable value per liquidation with excessive incentive?
validation_requirements: Set min > max, trigger liquidation, verify liquidator receives excess collateral
Calculate value extracted above intended maximum

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governor (intentionally or accidentally) sets minLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18[mode] > maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Liquidator targets positions in that mode
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Liquidation incentive exceeds max cap, giving liquidator more collateral than intended
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Position owner loses excess collateral beyond protocol safety parameters

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Position holders lose more than intended during liquidation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All liquidations in the affected mode

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: SetMinLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18 events with values exceeding maxLiqIncentiveMultiplier_e18
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large liquidation extractions in specific modes

threats.title: MoneyMarketHook Leftover WETH/ETH Sweepable by Any Caller via returnNative Flag
threats.hypothesis_description: If a user calls MoneyMarketHook.execute with returnNative=false and overpays msg.value during a WNATIVE repay operation (where repayAmt < msg.value), excess WETH remains in the hook contract. Any subsequent caller can invoke execute with empty parameters and returnNative=true to sweep all WETH/ETH held by the hook, stealing the leftover funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _handleRepay, WNATIVE deposits msg.value then reduces repayAmt by msg.value (or sets to 0 if msg.value > repayAmt). The excess stays as WETH in the hook. The execute function's returnNative path sweeps all WETH balance and all ETH balance to msg.sender, regardless of ownership. The hook has no per-user accounting for native token balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _handleRepay deposits msg.value as WETH but only uses repayAmt; execute returnNative sweeps entire WETH+ETH balance to msg.sender

preconditions: User overpays msg.value in a WNATIVE repay operation with returnNative=false
MEV bot or attacker monitors for WETH balance in the hook

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: returnNative flag sweeps entire balance without tracking ownership
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From the moment excess WETH is left until it's swept

operational_questions: How likely is overpayment in practice?
Can MEV bots atomically backrun overpayment transactions?
Is there any other mechanism that clears hook WETH balance?
validation_requirements: Send execute with WNATIVE repay and msg.value > repayAmt, returnNative=false
Verify WETH remains in hook
Call execute with empty params and returnNative=true from different address, verify sweep

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool for MoneyMarketHook.execute calls with msg.value > 0 and returnNative=false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Backrun the victim's transaction with execute({posId:0, mode:1, viewer:self, ..., returnNative:true})

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss of user funds left in the hook
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Minor - affects only users who overpay

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: WETH balance accumulation in MoneyMarketHook contract
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: execute calls with empty params and returnNative=true
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Backrun transactions after WNATIVE repay operations
